clinicalStem: A 32-year-old male primary school teacher presents with a 4-day history of hoarseness, mild sore throat, and low-grade fever (37.8°C) following a coryzal illness. He has no stridor, no dysphagia, no drooling, and no respiratory distress. Laryngoscopy shows diffuse erythema and oedema of both vocal cords without ulceration or exudate. His voice is markedly reduced but breath sounds are normal. He requests antibiotics to help him return to work quickly. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassure and advise voice rest, steam inhalation, and adequate hydration; prescribe antibiotics only if bacterial superinfection develops
Prescribe a 7-day course of amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily
Prescribe azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days
Refer urgently for direct laryngoscopy under general anaesthesia
Prescribe oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 5 days
explanation: Acute laryngitis following a coryzal illness is almost exclusively viral in aetiology; antibiotics provide no benefit and should not be routinely prescribed. Management is supportive: voice rest (the single most effective intervention), steam inhalation, adequate hydration, and avoidance of irritants such as smoking and whispering. Antibiotics are reserved for documented bacterial superinfection. Systemic steroids are only indicated for specific situations such as professional voice users with imminent performance obligations, not for routine acute laryngitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: viral acute laryngitis is self-limiting; supportive care is the cornerstone of management and antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of bacterial superinfection.
Amoxicillin is inappropriate because the cause is viral; prescribing antibiotics contributes to antimicrobial resistance and offers no clinical benefit in this scenario.
Azithromycin is not indicated; while it covers atypical organisms, the presentation is consistent with a post-viral viral laryngitis and does not warrant antibiotic therapy.

Urgent laryngoscopy under GA is unnecessary; there is no stridor, no respiratory compromise, no suspected foreign body or epiglottitis — elective or outpatient assessment is appropriate if symptoms persist beyond 3 weeks.

Systemic corticosteroids are reserved for professional voice users before a critical performance or cases of severe oedema compromising the airway; they are not indicated for routine acute viral laryngitis in a teacher without urgency.
clinicalPearl: Acute laryngitis is almost always viral — antibiotics are not indicated; voice rest is the most effective single treatment.
keyClue: Post-coryzal hoarseness with diffuse erythema, no exudate, no systemic toxicity — viral laryngitis
commonExamTrap: Prescribing amoxicillin because the patient is a professional voice user or the illness seems 'bacterial' without objective evidence of bacterial superinfection.
missedConcept: The routine use of antibiotics in acute laryngitis is not evidence-based; recognising viral aetiology prevents unnecessary antibiotic prescribing.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old male factory worker and heavy smoker (30 pack-years) presents with a 6-month history of persistent hoarseness, morning throat-clearing, and a dry cough. He drinks 5–6 units of alcohol per day. Flexible nasolaryngoscopy shows diffuse erythema, thickening of the vocal cord epithelium, and mild interarytenoid pachydermia. A biopsy shows hyperkeratosis without dysplasia. What single intervention is most likely to prevent progression of this condition to malignant change?
options: Complete cessation of smoking
Prescription of long-term proton pump inhibitor therapy
Regular 3-monthly laryngoscopic surveillance
Steam inhalation twice daily
Prescription of topical inhaled corticosteroids
explanation: Smoking is the single most important preventable risk factor for chronic laryngitis and its progression to laryngeal dysplasia and squamous cell carcinoma. Complete smoking cessation dramatically reduces the risk of malignant transformation and allows the mucosal changes to potentially regress. While GERD treatment and surveillance are important adjuncts, no intervention has a greater impact on preventing progression than stopping smoking.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: smoking is the dominant carcinogenic driver in this case; cessation is the most effective single preventive intervention against progression to dysplasia and cancer.

PPI therapy is useful if laryngopharyngeal reflux is a contributing factor, but the dominant risk here is smoking (30 pack-years); GERD treatment alone will not prevent progression driven by tobacco carcinogens.
Surveillance identifies progression but does not prevent it; the question asks for the intervention most likely to prevent malignant change, not to detect it.
Steam inhalation soothes symptoms but has no anti-carcinogenic effect and will not prevent malignant transformation.
Inhaled corticosteroids treat inflammatory or eosinophilic airway disease and have no role in preventing tobacco-induced mucosal malignant transformation.
clinicalPearl: In chronic laryngitis with hyperkeratosis, smoking cessation is the single most important intervention to prevent progression to laryngeal dysplasia and carcinoma.
keyClue: 30 pack-year smoker with persistent hoarseness and vocal cord hyperkeratosis — tobacco is the dominant preventable risk
commonExamTrap: Selecting PPI therapy as the primary prevention when tobacco exposure is clearly the dominant aetiological and carcinogenic factor.
missedConcept: Chronic laryngitis with leukoplakia/hyperkeratosis in a smoker is a pre-malignant condition; tobacco cessation is the primary preventive strategy.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department at 2 am by his parents. He has a 12-hour history of a barking cough, inspiratory stridor at rest, and moderate sternal recession. He is alert and able to drink. Temperature is 38.2°C, SpO2 96% on air, and respiratory rate is 38/min. He has no drooling and can handle secretions normally. On examination, the pharynx is mildly injected with no exudate. His neck is soft. What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological treatment?
options: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose
Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg
Intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg
Nebulised ipratropium bromide 250 mcg
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 40 mg/kg/day in divided doses
explanation: Croup (acute laryngotracheobronchitis) is caused by parainfluenza virus and produces subglottic oedema responsible for the barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and recession. Systemic corticosteroids — either oral dexamethasone (0.15–0.6 mg/kg) or oral prednisolone — are the mainstay of pharmacological treatment; they reduce subglottic oedema, decrease hospitalisation rates, and shorten illness duration. A single dose of oral dexamethasone is preferred for its long half-life and excellent bioavailability. Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe cases with impending airway obstruction.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: oral dexamethasone reduces the subglottic inflammatory oedema that drives stridor and recession in croup; a single dose is effective and safe.
Salbutamol is a beta-2 agonist used for bronchospasm; it has no role in the management of croup, which involves supraglottic/subglottic mucosal oedema, not lower airway bronchoconstriction.
IV ceftriaxone is inappropriate; croup is a viral illness and antibiotics have no role unless bacterial tracheitis or secondary bacterial infection is suspected.
Ipratropium bromide is an anticholinergic bronchodilator used in asthma and COPD; it has no role in croup management.
Amoxicillin-clavulanate is an antibiotic with no indication in viral croup; bacterial tracheitis presents differently (toxic appearance, failure to respond to steroids, high fever).
clinicalPearl: Oral dexamethasone is the first-line pharmacological treatment for moderate-to-severe croup; nebulised adrenaline is reserved for impending airway obstruction.
keyClue: Toddler with barking cough, inspiratory stridor at rest, and sternal recession — classic croup requiring corticosteroids
commonExamTrap: Selecting nebulised salbutamol as if croup were an asthma exacerbation; the two conditions are pathophysiologically distinct.
missedConcept: Croup is a viral subglottic condition treated with systemic corticosteroids, not bronchodilators or antibiotics.
redFlagReason: Inspiratory stridor at rest with sternal recession in a toddler indicates significant subglottic obstruction; if not treated promptly, respiratory failure and complete airway obstruction can develop.

topic: Laryngeal carcinoma — screening and early detection
clinicalStem: A 61-year-old male with a 40 pack-year smoking history and alcohol use of 60 units per week presents for a routine GP review. He has no current voice symptoms. He had a single episode of mild hoarseness 3 months ago that resolved spontaneously and which he attributed to 'a cold'. He has no dysphagia, odynophagia, otalgia, or weight loss. Examination is unremarkable. Which of the following statements best describes the appropriate approach to this patient with respect to laryngeal cancer risk?
options: Any hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks in a patient over 45 with smoking history should prompt urgent referral for laryngoscopy, regardless of spontaneous resolution
No action is required as hoarseness has resolved; reassure and review in 12 months
Arrange a CT neck with contrast to screen for laryngeal malignancy
Refer for laryngoscopy only if he develops persistent hoarseness lasting over 8 weeks
Prescribe a 4-week trial of PPI and reassess; refer if no improvement
explanation: NICE and international ENT guidelines recommend urgent 2-week-wait referral for laryngoscopy in any patient over 45 with unexplained persistent hoarseness, regardless of apparent resolution, when there is a significant smoking and alcohol history. Early-stage glottic tumours are highly curable (>90% 5-year survival for T1), but a brief resolution does not exclude an underlying lesion, as small tumours can cause intermittent symptoms. The prior episode of hoarseness in this high-risk patient warrants urgent laryngoscopic evaluation, not watchful waiting.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: in a high-risk patient (male, >45, heavy smoker, heavy drinker), even resolved hoarseness warrants urgent laryngoscopic evaluation because early-stage laryngeal cancer is highly curable if detected promptly.
Reassurance and 12-month review is inappropriate; this patient's risk profile demands proactive evaluation — delayed diagnosis of laryngeal cancer worsens prognosis significantly.
CT neck does not replace laryngoscopy as the first-line investigation for suspected laryngeal pathology; mucosal lesions, especially early T1 glottic tumours, may not be visible on CT.

An 8-week threshold is too long; guidelines specify that hoarseness persisting for more than 3 weeks in a high-risk patient over 45 warrants urgent referral — symptom resolution does not nullify the risk.
PPI trial is appropriate for laryngopharyngeal reflux as a cause of hoarseness, but should not delay urgent referral in a high-risk patient with significant smoking and alcohol exposure.
clinicalPearl: In patients over 45 with significant smoking/alcohol history, hoarseness of more than 3 weeks duration (even if transiently resolved) is a red flag mandating urgent laryngoscopy — early glottic cancer is highly curable.
keyClue: 61-year-old heavy smoker and heavy drinker with a prior episode of unexplained hoarseness — high-risk for laryngeal malignancy
commonExamTrap: Being falsely reassured by symptom resolution; early laryngeal cancers can cause intermittent hoarseness and a brief resolution does not exclude malignancy.
missedConcept: The 3-week hoarseness rule in patients over 45 with risk factors is a screening threshold for urgent laryngoscopy, not a sign-off criterion.

topic: Supraglottic carcinoma — clinical presentation and diagnosis
clinicalStem: A 67-year-old male ex-smoker (quit 5 years ago, prior 45 pack-years) presents with a 3-month history of progressive dysphagia to solids, a muffled 'hot potato' voice, and right-sided ear pain. He denies any hearing loss or otorrhoea. Examination of the right ear is entirely normal. Flexible nasolaryngoscopy shows a large, irregular, exophytic mass arising from the right aryepiglottic fold with pooling of secretions in the pyriform sinus. Cervical examination reveals a firm 3 cm node at right level II. What is the most likely primary tumour site?
options: Supraglottic larynx
Glottic larynx
Subglottic larynx
Hypopharynx (pyriform sinus)
Base of tongue
explanation: The mass arises from the aryepiglottic fold, which is part of the supraglottis. Supraglottic carcinomas classically present late with dysphagia, a muffled 'hot potato' voice (due to mass effect in the supraglottic larynx), referred otalgia (via Arnold's nerve — the auricular branch of CN X), and early nodal metastasis because the supraglottis has a rich bilateral lymphatic drainage. The normal ear examination with ipsilateral otalgia is the classic pattern of referred pain. The pooling in the pyriform sinus is secondary to dysphagia and supraglottic obstruction, not a primary pyriform sinus tumour.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: the mass on the aryepiglottic fold localises to the supraglottis; the combination of dysphagia, muffled voice, referred otalgia with a normal ear, and early nodal disease is the supraglottic carcinoma signature.

Glottic tumours arise from the true vocal cords and present early with hoarseness; they have sparse lymphatic drainage and rarely present with large nodal metastases or dysphagia as the first symptom.
Subglottic tumours are rare, arise below the true cords, and typically present with progressive stridor; referred otalgia and dysphagia are not typical features.

Pyriform sinus is part of the hypopharynx, not the larynx; while pooling is seen in the pyriform sinus, the mass itself is described as arising from the aryepiglottic fold, which is supraglottic larynx.
Base-of-tongue tumours cause dysphagia and can refer pain via CN IX (glossopharyngeal nerve); the mass is described on the aryepiglottic fold (supraglottic), not the tongue base.
clinicalPearl: Ipsilateral otalgia with a completely normal ear examination is referred pain via Arnold's nerve (auricular branch of vagus) — always examine the larynx and hypopharynx for a primary tumour.
keyClue: Mass on aryepiglottic fold, muffled voice, referred otalgia with normal ear, early nodal disease — supraglottic carcinoma
commonExamTrap: Localising the primary to the pyriform sinus because of secretion pooling — pooling is secondary to the supraglottic mass, not primary hypopharyngeal pathology.
missedConcept: Supraglottic cancers present late with dysphagia and nodal disease; referred otalgia with a normal ear is a hallmark of supraglottic/pharyngeal malignancy via Arnold's nerve.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old male construction worker attends a health promotion clinic. He smokes 20 cigarettes per day, drinks 35 units of alcohol per week, and works in an asbestos-containing environment without consistent use of respiratory protective equipment. He has no current ENT symptoms. His father died of oropharyngeal cancer. He asks what he can do to most effectively reduce his personal risk of developing laryngeal cancer. Ranked in order of impact, which combination of interventions represents the most important preventive measures?
options: Smoking cessation as the highest priority, followed by reduction of alcohol intake, and consistent use of appropriate respiratory protection at work
Alcohol abstinence as the highest priority, followed by smoking cessation, with occupational exposure being low priority
HPV vaccination as the primary prevention strategy, combined with smoking cessation
Annual laryngoscopy screening as the primary intervention, combined with dietary antioxidant supplementation
Smoking reduction (halving cigarettes) combined with switching to low-tar cigarettes and regular GP reviews
explanation: Tobacco smoking is the single most important risk factor for laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma, accounting for the vast majority of cases; cessation is the highest priority intervention. Alcohol acts synergistically with tobacco to multiply risk severalfold. Occupational exposure to asbestos, wood dust, and industrial chemicals is a recognised but quantitatively lesser risk factor. The combined effect of stopping smoking, reducing alcohol, and removing occupational carcinogen exposure represents a comprehensive primary prevention strategy. HPV vaccination is relevant for oropharyngeal cancer but has limited evidence as a primary prevention for laryngeal SCC.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: tobacco is the dominant risk factor for laryngeal carcinoma; alcohol is synergistic; occupational exposure to asbestos is a recognised additional risk — addressing all three constitutes optimal primary prevention.
Although alcohol is a significant co-carcinogen, tobacco smoking has a larger independent attributable risk for laryngeal carcinoma; smoking cessation must be ranked first in any preventive strategy.
HPV (types 16 and 18) is strongly linked to oropharyngeal (base of tongue, tonsil) cancer in non-smokers; its role in laryngeal SCC is far less established than for oropharyngeal sites.
Annual laryngoscopy is a surveillance strategy (secondary prevention), not a primary preventive intervention; dietary antioxidants have no established evidence base for preventing laryngeal cancer.
Smoking reduction or switching to low-tar cigarettes does not eliminate carcinogenic exposure; only complete cessation meaningfully reduces laryngeal cancer risk.
clinicalPearl: Tobacco and alcohol act synergistically in laryngeal cancer — cessation of smoking is the single most impactful primary preventive intervention.
keyClue: Heavy smoker and drinker with occupational asbestos exposure — tobacco is the dominant modifiable risk
commonExamTrap: Ranking alcohol above tobacco in prevention priority for laryngeal cancer; while synergistic, tobacco has the larger independent attributable risk.
missedConcept: Primary prevention of laryngeal cancer centres on tobacco cessation; alcohol and occupational carcinogens are important but secondary risk factors.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old female university student presents with a 2-day history of severe sore throat, odynophagia, and fever of 38.9°C. She has no cough. On examination, she has tonsillar exudate bilaterally, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and no pharyngeal vesicles. Rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for Group A beta-haemolytic Streptococcus (GABHS) is positive. She has no drug allergies. What is the most appropriate antibiotic choice?
options: Phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 500 mg twice daily for 10 days
Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 7 days
Co-amoxiclav 625 mg three times daily for 7 days
Azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 5 days
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 5 days
explanation: Confirmed GABHS pharyngitis (positive RADT with Centor score 4: exudate, tender anterior nodes, fever, no cough) warrants antibiotic treatment primarily to prevent suppurative complications (peritonsillar abscess, otitis media) and acute rheumatic fever. Phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) remains the first-line antibiotic of choice for GABHS pharyngitis; GABHS has never demonstrated resistance to penicillin. A 10-day course is recommended to achieve microbiological eradication and maximally reduce rheumatic fever risk. Amoxicillin should be avoided as first-line if infectious mononucleosis has not been excluded, due to the risk of a maculopapular rash.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: penicillin V is the first-line agent for GABHS pharyngitis — it is narrow-spectrum, safe, inexpensive, and GABHS is uniformly susceptible; a 10-day course reduces suppurative complications and rheumatic fever.

Amoxicillin is not recommended as first-line because if infectious mononucleosis (EBV) is present and unrecognised, amoxicillin causes a florid maculopapular rash in >90% of cases; penicillin V is preferred.
Co-amoxiclav is a broad-spectrum combination antibiotic not indicated as first-line for uncomplicated GABHS pharyngitis; its use promotes unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure.
Azithromycin is reserved for penicillin-allergic patients; rising macrolide resistance rates (10–20%) in GABHS in many regions make it a second-line option.

Ciprofloxacin (a fluoroquinolone) has poor activity against GABHS, is not indicated for pharyngitis, and should be reserved for serious gram-negative infections; its use here would be inappropriate stewardship.
clinicalPearl: Penicillin V for 10 days is the first-line treatment for confirmed GABHS pharyngitis; avoid amoxicillin until EBV mononucleosis is excluded due to rash risk.
keyClue: Centor score 4 with positive RADT — confirmed GABHS requiring 10-day penicillin V
commonExamTrap: Choosing amoxicillin instead of penicillin V — amoxicillin risks a florid rash if the patient has unrecognised EBV mononucleosis.
missedConcept: GABHS remains uniformly sensitive to penicillin; the choice of penicillin V over amoxicillin avoids the EBV-amoxicillin rash risk and promotes narrow-spectrum prescribing.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of sore throat, mild fever (37.9°C), coryza, hoarseness, and a dry cough. On examination, the oropharynx is mildly erythematous without exudate. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy. Her Centor score is 1. Rapid antigen detection test for GABHS is negative. She is otherwise healthy with no drug allergies and asks for antibiotics to resolve her symptoms faster. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Explain that antibiotics will not hasten recovery, prescribe simple analgesics and adequate hydration, and advise to return if symptoms worsen or persist beyond 7–10 days
Prescribe phenoxymethylpenicillin 500 mg twice daily for 5 days to prevent complications
Prescribe azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days to cover atypical organisms
Arrange a throat swab culture and prescribe antibiotics pending the result
Prescribe amoxicillin-clavulanate 625 mg three times daily for 7 days
explanation: This presentation is consistent with viral pharyngitis (Centor score 1, negative RADT, coryza, cough, hoarseness — features atypical for bacterial disease). Antibiotics provide no meaningful symptom benefit and do not prevent complications in viral pharyngitis. Management centres on patient education, symptomatic relief with NSAIDs or paracetamol, adequate hydration, and a safety-net advice to return if symptoms worsen. Avoiding unnecessary antibiotics is a key public health and antimicrobial stewardship priority.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: viral pharyngitis is self-limiting; antibiotics offer no clinical benefit; symptomatic management with a safety net is the evidence-based approach and promotes antibiotic stewardship.
Prescribing penicillin V for a Centor score of 1 with a negative RADT is not justified; guidelines recommend antibiotics only for high-risk patients (Centor ≥3–4 or positive RADT).
Azithromycin has no indication in viral pharyngitis; using macrolides unnecessarily promotes resistance and offers no benefit over symptomatic care.
Throat culture is not indicated when the clinical presentation is clearly viral (Centor 1, negative RADT, viral features); prescribing antibiotics pending culture drives unnecessary prescribing.
Co-amoxiclav is a broad-spectrum antibiotic with no indication in viral pharyngitis; its use represents significant inappropriate prescribing and antibiotic misuse.
clinicalPearl: Viral pharyngitis (Centor ≤2, negative RADT, cough/coryza present) should be managed symptomatically; antibiotics are not indicated and should not be prescribed.
keyClue: Centor score 1, negative RADT, coryza, hoarseness, cough — viral pharyngitis, not bacterial
commonExamTrap: Prescribing antibiotics because the patient requests them; clinical features clearly indicate a viral aetiology that does not benefit from antibiotics.
missedConcept: The Centor score and RADT are tools to restrict antibiotic use to high-probability bacterial pharyngitis; a Centor score of 1 with viral features should not be treated with antibiotics.

topic: Peritonsillar abscess — emergency recognition and management
clinicalStem: A 22-year-old male presents with a 5-day history of severe sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and fever of 39.4°C. He has been on amoxicillin for 3 days prescribed by his GP for tonsillitis without improvement. Today he developed trismus (mouth opening restricted to 2 cm), a change in voice quality described as 'hot potato', and is drooling. Examination reveals uvular deviation to the left, bulging of the right soft palate and right tonsillar area, and the right tonsil appears displaced medially. He is in significant distress. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Urgent needle aspiration or incision and drainage of the peritonsillar abscess, with intravenous antibiotics and analgesia
Change oral antibiotic to co-amoxiclav and discharge with close outpatient follow-up in 48 hours
Immediate tonsillectomy under general anaesthesia
CT neck with contrast to confirm the diagnosis before any drainage procedure
Increase amoxicillin dose and add oral metronidazole; reassess in 24 hours
explanation: The clinical picture is diagnostic of a peritonsillar abscess (quinsy): uvular deviation away from the affected side, unilateral soft palate bulging with medial tonsillar displacement, trismus, 'hot potato' voice, drooling, and failure to improve on oral antibiotics. This is a surgical emergency requiring urgent drainage (needle aspiration or incision and drainage) combined with intravenous antibiotics (benzylpenicillin + metronidazole, or co-amoxiclav) and adequate analgesia. Delayed drainage risks spontaneous rupture into the airway, retropharyngeal spread, or descending necrotising mediastinitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: peritonsillar abscess with trismus and uvular deviation requires urgent surgical drainage; IV antibiotics alone will not resolve an established abscess and delay exposes the patient to life-threatening complications.

Switching to oral co-amoxiclav and discharging is dangerous; an established abscess will not resolve with antibiotics alone — drainage is essential and delay risks airway compromise and mediastinal spread.

Immediate tonsillectomy under GA (quinsy tonsillectomy) is occasionally performed but is not the standard first-line procedure; needle aspiration or I&D under local anaesthesia is faster and equally effective for immediate management.

CT neck may be obtained if deep neck space extension is suspected, but the classic clinical signs of peritonsillar abscess are sufficient to proceed with drainage without mandatory CT — delaying drainage for imaging in a patient with trismus and impending airway risk is inappropriate.

Increasing the amoxicillin dose and waiting 24 hours is dangerous; amoxicillin does not cover anaerobes (key organisms in peritonsillar abscess), and an established abscess requires surgical drainage, not escalation of the same antibiotic.
clinicalPearl: Peritonsillar abscess = uvular deviation + unilateral palatal bulging + trismus + hot potato voice — this is a surgical emergency requiring drainage, not further antibiotics alone.
keyClue: Uvular deviation to the left with right-sided peritonsillar bulging and trismus — classic peritonsillar abscess requiring urgent drainage
commonExamTrap: Switching antibiotics without draining the abscess; an established collection cannot be resolved with antibiotics alone and delay risks airway compromise.
missedConcept: Peritonsillar abscess is distinguished from severe tonsillitis by uvular deviation, peritonsillar bulging, trismus, and failure to respond to antibiotics — drainage is mandatory.
redFlagReason: Untreated peritonsillar abscess can rupture into the airway causing aspiration, or spread to the retropharyngeal space and descend as necrotising mediastinitis — a life-threatening emergency.

topic: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma — screening in high-risk populations
subtopic: EBV serology as a screening tool
clinicalStem: A 42-year-old Cantonese-speaking man from Guangdong province, China, who migrated to the UK 15 years ago, attends his GP for a routine health check. He has no ENT symptoms. He is a non-smoker and light drinker. He mentions that his father and paternal uncle both died of nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC). His examination is entirely normal. He asks whether he can be screened for NPC. Which is the most appropriate screening strategy for this high-risk individual?
options: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) serology — specifically VCA-IgA and EA-IgA antibody titres — with consideration of nasopharyngoscopy if elevated
Annual plain X-ray of the nasopharynx and skull base
Annual MRI brain and skull base as primary screening
Serum squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) antigen and CEA as primary tumour markers
No screening is available or validated; advise the patient that surveillance is not recommended outside clinical trials
explanation: EBV is the aetiological agent of nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC), particularly the undifferentiated type most prevalent in Southern Chinese populations. In high-risk populations (Southern Chinese ethnicity, family history, EBV exposure), serological screening using EBV viral capsid antigen IgA (VCA-IgA) and early antigen IgA (EA-IgA) is the validated approach used in endemic regions such as Hong Kong and Guangdong. Elevated titres prompt nasopharyngoscopy with biopsy. Plasma EBV DNA is also used in surveillance. This strategy has detected NPC at early stages in mass screening programmes in endemic areas with improved survival outcomes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: EBV VCA-IgA and EA-IgA are validated serological screening tools for NPC in high-risk (Southern Chinese) populations; elevated titres trigger nasopharyngoscopic evaluation.
Plain X-ray has no validated role in NPC screening; early nasopharyngeal mucosal lesions are not visible on plain X-ray and the method lacks both sensitivity and specificity.
Annual MRI is too expensive, lacks specificity for population-based screening, and is not validated as a primary NPC screening modality; it is used for staging once diagnosis is made.
SCC antigen and CEA are not validated screening tools for NPC; EBV serology is the accepted biomarker for screening in endemic/high-risk populations.

Validated screening strategies do exist for high-risk populations using EBV serology; telling this high-risk patient with a family history that no screening is available is incorrect and deprives him of a recognised screening opportunity.
clinicalPearl: EBV VCA-IgA and EA-IgA serology are the validated screening tools for nasopharyngeal carcinoma in high-risk Southern Chinese populations with family history.
keyClue: Southern Chinese ethnicity, family history of NPC, asymptomatic — EBV serological screening is appropriate
commonExamTrap: Concluding that no screening exists for NPC; in the high-risk Southern Chinese population, EBV serological screening is a validated and practised strategy.
missedConcept: NPC has a strong EBV association in endemic populations; EBV serology (VCA-IgA, EA-IgA) serves as a cost-effective population screening tool in high-risk groups.

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old boy's parents attend a GP appointment asking about the HPV vaccine. The boy has not yet received it. His GP notes that oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (OPSCC) rates are rising sharply in young males and that HPV-positive OPSCC now accounts for the majority of oropharyngeal cancers in developed countries. The parents ask whether the vaccine will protect against throat cancer as well as genital warts and cervical cancer. What is the most accurate response?
options: The HPV vaccine (particularly the nonavalent vaccine) protects against HPV types 16 and 18, which cause the majority of HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinomas; vaccination is recommended and most effective before sexual debut
The HPV vaccine only protects against genital warts and cervical cancer; it has no demonstrated protection against oropharyngeal cancer
Oropharyngeal cancer is predominantly caused by tobacco and alcohol; HPV is a minor factor and vaccination will have little impact on oropharyngeal cancer rates
HPV vaccination is recommended only for females; males do not develop HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer
Vaccination is ineffective once the patient is aged 16; it should only be administered before the age of 12
explanation: HPV types 16 and 18 are the causative agents in approximately 70–80% of HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas (predominantly base of tongue and tonsil). The nonavalent HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) targets HPV 16, 18, and seven other types. Vaccination before sexual debut provides near-complete protection against infection with these high-risk types. Epidemiological modelling and immunogenicity data support vaccination of adolescent males as an important public health strategy to reduce future OPSCC burden. The vaccine is routinely offered to both sexes in the UK national programme.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: HPV 16/18 are responsible for the majority of HPV-positive OPSCC; the nonavalent vaccine provides protection against these types and is most effective when administered before first sexual exposure.

This is incorrect; while the vaccine was initially developed for cervical cancer, evidence supports protection against HPV-16/18-driven oropharyngeal carcinoma through prevention of initial HPV infection.

In non-smokers and young adults, HPV is now the dominant aetiological factor for oropharyngeal SCC in developed countries, surpassing tobacco and alcohol; vaccination has significant potential to reduce OPSCC rates.

HPV vaccination is recommended for both sexes in the UK; HPV-positive OPSCC disproportionately affects males (3:1 male:female ratio), making male vaccination particularly important for head and neck cancer prevention.

While the vaccine is most immunogenic when given to pre-adolescents, it remains beneficial and is licensed up to age 26 (and in some guidelines up to 45 in certain risk groups); age 16 is not beyond the window of benefit.
clinicalPearl: HPV vaccination (nonavalent) protects against HPV 16 and 18 — the causative agents of the majority of oropharyngeal SCC; male vaccination is essential as OPSCC disproportionately affects men.
keyClue: Rising HPV-positive OPSCC, 16-year-old male, parents asking about protection — nonavalent HPV vaccine covers the causative serotypes
commonExamTrap: Stating that HPV vaccination only protects females or only applies to genital/cervical disease; HPV 16/18 drive oropharyngeal carcinoma in both sexes.
missedConcept: HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma is now the most common HPV-related cancer in developed countries; vaccination of adolescent males is a primary prevention priority.

topic: Hypopharyngeal carcinoma — diagnosis and red flags
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old male presents with a 4-month history of progressive dysphagia, initially to solids then liquids, 8 kg unintentional weight loss, and left-sided referred otalgia. He is a 35 pack-year smoker and drinks 40 units of alcohol per week. He also reports a globus sensation and a 'feeling of food sticking' at the level of the lower throat. Flexible nasolaryngoscopy shows pooling of secretions in the left pyriform sinus with mucosal irregularity, but the vocal cords appear mobile. A firm 2.5 cm left level II lymph node is palpable. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Carcinoma of the left pyriform sinus (hypopharyngeal carcinoma)
Laryngeal carcinoma with hypopharyngeal extension
Zenker's diverticulum
Oesophageal carcinoma at the cricopharyngeus
Retropharyngeal abscess
explanation: The combination of progressive dysphagia in a heavy smoker and drinker, pyriform sinus pooling with mucosal irregularity, mobile vocal cords (distinguishing from T3–T4 laryngeal invasion), referred otalgia, and level II nodal metastasis is classic for pyriform sinus (hypopharyngeal) carcinoma. Hypopharyngeal SCC is notoriously silent until advanced; most patients present with stage III–IV disease. The pyriform sinus is the most common subsite of hypopharyngeal cancer and is strongly associated with tobacco and alcohol. Mobile cords indicate that the tumour has not invaded the paraglottic space or cricoarytenoid joint.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: pooling in the left pyriform sinus with mucosal irregularity, mobile cords, referred otalgia, and level II nodal disease in a heavy smoker/drinker is the classic pyriform sinus carcinoma presentation.

Laryngeal carcinoma may extend to the hypopharynx, but the primary finding here is pyriform sinus involvement with mobile vocal cords; vocal cord fixation would suggest laryngeal carcinoma with cricoarytenoid involvement.

Zenker's diverticulum causes dysphagia, regurgitation of undigested food, and gurgling, but does not cause lymphadenopathy, pyriform sinus mucosal irregularity, or referred otalgia; it is not associated with malignancy of the pyriform sinus.

Cricopharyngeal oesophageal carcinoma would be seen at or below the postcricoid region on nasolaryngoscopy; the mucosal irregularity described is in the pyriform sinus, which is a distinct hypopharyngeal subsite.

Retropharyngeal abscess causes neck pain, dysphagia, fever, and bulging of the posterior pharyngeal wall; it does not cause pyriform sinus mucosal irregularity, persistent dysphagia over 4 months, or level II nodal metastasis.
clinicalPearl: Pyriform sinus carcinoma presents late with pooling of secretions, progressive dysphagia, referred otalgia, and early nodal spread — mobile cords exclude cricoarytenoid invasion at this stage.
keyClue: Pyriform sinus pooling with mucosal irregularity, mobile cords, level II node, heavy smoker/drinker — pyriform sinus SCC
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing laryngeal carcinoma because of hoarseness-like symptoms; mobile vocal cords and the pyriform sinus as the primary site of mucosal change favour hypopharyngeal SCC.
missedConcept: Hypopharyngeal SCC (pyriform sinus) presents late and silently; secretion pooling on nasolaryngoscopy is a key diagnostic clue.
